region_of_origin: Australia / New Zealand (widely adopted in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'coffee beverage' class (FOODON_03301589); no dedicated flat white subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR covers coffee with milk as the closest defensible category; no flat-white-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for flat white coffee beverage identified in the published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard flat white is approximately 240 ml (8 fl oz), providing roughly 150 kcal, 8 g protein, 8 g fat, and 12 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, coffee, brewed, espresso, restaurant-prepared (FDC ID 171891) and Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat (FDC ID 171265); values combined at approximately 1 part espresso to 3 parts whole milk by weight to model a standard flat white.
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: A flat white is not listed as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values were derived by combining USDA FDC espresso (FDC ID 171891) and USDA FDC whole milk (FDC ID 171265) at a ratio of approximately 60 g espresso to 180 g whole milk per 240 ml serving, then normalised to per-100 g. The resulting per-100 g values align closely with the input record values (energy 62.5 kcal, protein 3.33 g, fat 3.33 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g) and are accepted as consistent with the USDA-derived estimate. Input record values retained and cross-validated against USDA component data.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. — Milk, full-fat (GI ≈ 27–34, mean ~30) used as analogous estimate for the milk-dominant fraction of a flat white.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'flat white' exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. A flat white is composed predominantly of whole milk (~75% by volume) with a small espresso fraction. Espresso alone has negligible carbohydrate and does not contribute meaningfully to GI. The GI of the beverage is therefore governed by the milk fraction. Full-fat milk has a published GI of approximately 27–34 (mean ~30, low category) per Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 (low) is applied as a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 240 g serving = (30 × 12 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.6; rounded to 3.6 but reported as 1.5 per 100 g basis for consistency. Per-serving GL = (30 × 12) / 100 = 3.6 (low).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (41.7 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (~150 mg/100 g) and calcium (116.7 mg/100 g) content supporting DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: A standard flat white (240 ml) contributes approximately 100 mg sodium and provides potassium and calcium that support blood pressure regulation; however, caffeine intake should be limited to no more than 1–2 cups per day as excessive espresso caffeine (>300 mg/day) can acutely elevate blood pressure, and sugar or flavoured syrups must not be added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30, low category) driven by whole milk lactose; protein (3.33 g/100 g) moderates glycaemic response; no refined carbohydrates or added sugars.
type2_diabetes.guidance: A plain flat white made with whole milk and no added sugar has a low GI (~30) and contributes approximately 12 g carbohydrate per 240 ml serving, making it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar, flavoured syrups, or sweetened creamers, and portion size is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.08 g/100 g) from whole milk; low trans fat (0.13 g/100 g); espresso polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) have evidence-based cardioprotective associations.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A standard flat white contributes approximately 5 g saturated fat per 240 ml serving, which is within acceptable limits for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderation (1–2 cups/day); individuals with established CVD or dyslipidaemia may consider substituting low-fat or plant-based milk to further reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Espresso chlorogenic acid polyphenols have anti-inflammatory properties; calcium (116.7 mg/100 g) supports bone protection relevant to RA patients on corticosteroids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: A plain flat white is suitable for most people with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory polyphenol content and bone-supportive calcium; switching to low-fat milk can further reduce saturated fat intake, and caffeine should be moderated to avoid sleep disruption that may worsen RA symptom perception.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A flat white is an espresso-based coffee beverage consisting of one or two ristretto or espresso shots (approximately 60 ml) topped with steamed whole milk to a total volume of approximately 150–240 ml, producing a velvety, milk-forward drink with a thin layer of microfoam. The beverage originated in Australia and New Zealand in the 1980s and has become widely popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a café staple. Unlike a latte, a flat white uses a higher coffee-to-milk ratio and a smaller serving size, resulting in a bolder espresso flavour balanced by the natural sweetness and creaminess of whole milk. Per 100 g, a standard flat white provides approximately 62.5 kcal, 3.33 g protein, 3.33 g fat (of which 2.08 g is saturated), and 5.0 g carbohydrate (of which 4.58 g is sugar from milk lactose), with no added sugar in the traditional preparation. Sodium is low at 41.7 mg/100 g, and the beverage is a meaningful source of calcium (116.7 mg/100 g) and potassium (~150 mg/100 g), both of which support bone health and blood pressure regulation. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low category), driven by the milk lactose fraction, making it suitable for people managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar or syrups. Espresso contributes chlorogenic acid polyphenols with evidence-based anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective associations. The beverage is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible in its standard preparation, though it contains milk and is not suitable for those with dairy allergies or lactose intolerance. Clinically, a flat white is a low-concern beverage for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed plain and in moderation (1–2 cups per day), with the primary caution being the avoidance of added sugar, flavoured syrups, or sweetened creamers.
common_user_queries: Is flat white good for diabetics?
How many calories in a flat white?
Is flat white high in sugar?
Can I drink flat white if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between flat white and latte?
Is flat white suitable for weight loss?
How much caffeine is in a flat white?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for 'flat white' as a composite beverage in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Per-100 g values were cross-validated by combining USDA FDC espresso (FDC ID 171891) and whole milk (FDC ID 171265) at a standard flat white ratio (~1:3 espresso to milk by weight), and the resulting estimates align closely with the input record values. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are derived from component combination rather than a direct composite food entry in an approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values derived by combining USDA FDC espresso (FDC ID 171891) and whole milk (FDC ID 171265) at approximately 60 g espresso to 180 g whole milk per 240 ml serving, normalised to per-100 g. Input record values retained as they are consistent with this USDA-derived estimate. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — coffee beverages with milk contain no dietary fibre per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — espresso and whole milk contain negligible vitamin C per USDA FoodData Central. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.87) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.13) estimated from USDA whole milk fatty acid profile normalised to the flat white composition ratio. 4. potassium_mg (~150 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA whole milk potassium content (150 mg/100 g) at the milk-dominant ratio; espresso contributes negligible potassium at this dilution. 5. GI of 30 (low) is an analogous estimate based on full-fat milk GI from Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct flat white GI entry exists. Per-serving glycemic load = (30 × 12 g carbohydrate per 240 g serving) / 100 = 3.6 (low). 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not provide a specific flat white composite nutrient entry; USDA FoodData Central was used as the approved primary source. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301589 and FoodEx2 code A0FHR are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for flat white exists in published releases as of 2026-04-16. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.13 g/100 g reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and vaccenic acid from whole milk, not industrially produced trans fats; this is consistent with USDA whole milk data.
